lesson.title: Welcome to PY101!
masteryOutcomes: Meet Maestro and understand how learning works here: short conversations, real code, one climb at a time.
Type a line of Python in the editor, press Run, and read the output.
Make the computer print your own words — including the dream you shared with Maestro.

concept: Your first conversation with Maestro and your first real line of Python: how this course works, and making the computer speak your words with print().

explanations.text: Coding is writing instructions a computer follows. Python is the friendliest language for it. print() is your first instruction: whatever you put between the quotes, the computer says out loud in the output panel.

In this course you learn by doing: I explain one small idea, you type real Python in the editor on this page and press Run, and we look at what actually happened together. Your first tool is print("...") — it displays any text you choose. The parentheses hand the text to print; the quotes mark where your words start and end.

print() is a function: a named action the computer performs. The text in quotes is a string — a piece of data. When you press Run, Python reads your file top to bottom and executes each instruction in order, so two print lines produce two lines of output, in the order you wrote them.

examples: Grade math: 10% + 10% + 10% (unit reviews) + 60% (final review) + 10% (discussions) = 100%, plus 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time.

hintLadder.text: Everything starts with the word print, then parentheses right after it.
Inside the parentheses, put your words between quotes: print("like this").
Type exactly: print("Hello, PY101!") — then press Run and read the output panel.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes print without quotes, e.g. print(Hello)
misconceptions.correction: Without quotes Python looks for a variable named Hello and raises a NameError. Quotes tell Python these are your words, not code: print("Hello").

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks the code runs somewhere else or needs installing Python
misconceptions.correction: Everything runs right here in the editor on this page — type, press Run, and the output appears below. Nothing to install, nothing leaves your device.

checks.prompt: Type print("Hello, PY101!") in the editor, press Run, and tell me exactly what appeared in the output.
acceptanceCriteria: Mentions that the text Hello, PY101! appeared in the output
Shows they actually ran the code rather than guessing
checks.passFeedback: That's it — you just gave a computer an instruction and it obeyed. That's coding.
checks.failFeedback: Look at the output panel right under the editor — read me exactly what it shows.

checks.prompt: Now the real one: change the text inside the quotes so the computer prints YOUR dream — the one you told me. Run it and tell me what it says.
acceptanceCriteria: Edited the string inside print() to their own words
Reports the printed output matching what they wrote
checks.passFeedback: Your own dream, spoken by your own code. Remember this moment — every app you'll ever build starts exactly like this.
checks.failFeedback: Keep the print("...") shape and only change the words between the quotes — then Run.

practice.prompt: Make the computer print your dream in your own words.
acceptanceCriteria: The string is their own text

practice.prompt: In one sentence: what does print() do, and why do the quotes matter?
rubric: Mentions that print displays text in the output, and that quotes mark the exact text (their words, not code).

practice.prompt: Which line correctly prints the word Climb?
practice.explanation: print needs parentheses, and your words need quotes — print("Climb").

pedagogyRules: Get the learner to their first successful Run within the first three turns — action before theory.
Use the learner's own dream text as the material for the second print — never substitute a generic example.
If they hit NameError from missing quotes, celebrate it as their first real error and show the one-character-class fix.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm the student's grading summary if the numbers are exactly right: 10% each for reviews 1-3, 60% for the timed final with one retake, 10% for discussions, 10 bonus points for on-time passes. If any number is off, correct that specific number — do not wave it through.
Do not dump the whole course map and grading scheme at once. Introduce the four units first, check understanding with one light question, then layer in the grading rules one piece at a time.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student demotivated or intimidated before starting
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Name the fear kindly, then point at the design: the first unit is literally writing one line of code, and the first three reviews are untimed with unlimited retakes — the course is built so beginners cannot fall through the cracks. Then give the tiniest next action.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student wants to skip orientation entirely
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Don't force the full tour. Give the compressed version — four units, 10-10-10-60-10 grading, timed final with one retake, bonus for on-time passes — verify with one quick question, and advance to writing their first program.

chunks.text: Learning here is a conversation: Maestro explains one small idea at a time, you type real Python in the editor on this page and press Run, and you look at the real output together. Everything runs on your own device — free, private, nothing to install.

print("...") is the instruction that makes the computer display text. The parentheses hand your text to print; the quotes mark exactly where your words begin and end. print("Hello, PY101!") shows: Hello, PY101!
print(Hello) without quotes raises NameError, because Python thinks Hello is a variable name. Errors are clues, not failures: read the last line, fix the exact piece it names, run again.

PY101 climbs in small steps: first making the computer speak (print), then storing values, then decisions and loops. Each lesson ends when you have shown you can do the thing — then you move up the mountain.

celebration.headline: The computer just spoke your words.
celebration.recap: You wrote your first real Python, ran it, and made the machine print your own dream. That is not a toy — it is the exact skill every program is built from.
celebration.unlocked: Next: Writing your first program — stacking instructions and making the output truly yours.

summaryBullets: You can now write a real Python instruction in the editor, press Run, and read the output.
You can now use print("...") to make the computer say any words you choose — you made it say your dream.
You can now read an error like NameError as a clue and fix exactly what it points at.
You know how this climb works: one small idea, real code, and up we go.
openingLine: Hey {name} — welcome to the mountain. Before any theory, I want you to feel the whole magic of coding in the next five minutes: you're going to make this computer say something that matters to you. Ready?

turns.text: honestly I have no idea what coding even is
Perfect starting point. Coding is writing instructions a computer follows — and you'll give your first one right now. Type print("hi") in the editor and press Run. What shows up?
You just commanded a computer and it obeyed. That's the whole game — everything else is bigger versions of what you just did. Ready to make it say something that matters to you?
print(Hello) should work, quotes are just decoration
Run it and watch — you'll get a NameError. Without quotes, Python hunts for a variable named Hello. The quotes say: these are my words, not code. Try print("Hello") — what changes?
oh wow yeah, NameError... and with quotes it just says Hello
why start with print, seems useless

Every app you've ever used shows you text somewhere — print is the seed of that. It's also how coders see inside their programs. Make it print your name, then tell me what you'd want an app of yours to say.
ok fair, it printed my name
